mistakes.incorrect: I have experience on managing IT projects.
mistakes.correct: I have experience in managing IT projects.
mistakes.note: The standard preposition for professional experience is 'in' [1].

mistakes.incorrect: He applied to a new job yesterday.
mistakes.correct: He applied for a new job yesterday.
mistakes.note: Use 'apply for' when referring to a position or job [2, 3].

mistakes.incorrect: She is in charge with the marketing team.
mistakes.correct: She is in charge of the marketing team.
mistakes.note: The fixed phrase is 'in charge of' [4, 5].

mistakes.incorrect: I am responsible to managing online campaigns.
mistakes.correct: I am responsible for managing online campaigns.
mistakes.note: Use 'responsible for' followed by a noun or V-ing [4].

mistakes.incorrect: He graduated at a prestigious university.
mistakes.correct: He graduated from a prestigious university.
mistakes.note: The verb 'graduate' always takes the preposition 'from' [6, 7].

mistakes.incorrect: The manager was relieved with his duties.
mistakes.correct: The manager was relieved of his duties.
mistakes.note: To be removed from a position, use 'relieved of' [8].

mistakes.incorrect: I have worked at an international NGO.
mistakes.correct: I have worked for an international NGO.
mistakes.note: When talking about an employer, 'work for' is more common than 'work at' [1].

mistakes.incorrect: According with the news, climate change is alarming.
mistakes.correct: According to the news, climate change is alarming.
mistakes.note: The fixed phrase is 'according to' [6].

mistakes.incorrect: Global warming has a negative impact to the arctic.
mistakes.correct: Global warming has a negative impact on the arctic.
mistakes.note: 'Impact' and 'effect' take the preposition 'on' [9, 10].

mistakes.incorrect: We must contribute for protecting biodiversity.
mistakes.correct: We must contribute to protecting biodiversity.
mistakes.note: The verb 'contribute' is followed by 'to' [11, 12].

mistakes.incorrect: Factories should limit emissions of greenhouse gas.
mistakes.correct: Factories should curb emissions of greenhouse gas.
mistakes.note: 'Curb' is a stronger, more academic collocation for emissions [11, 13].

mistakes.incorrect: We need to protect endangered species against harm.
mistakes.correct: We need to protect endangered species from harm.
mistakes.note: Use 'protect someone/something from' [14].

mistakes.incorrect: How can we cope for environmental problems?
mistakes.correct: How can we cope with environmental problems?
mistakes.note: The correct phrasal verb is 'cope with' [3, 13].

mistakes.incorrect: Deforestation is a serious threat for wildlife.
mistakes.correct: Deforestation is a serious threat to wildlife.
mistakes.note: Use 'threat to' when describing danger [15, 16].

mistakes.incorrect: I need to make my homework before class.
mistakes.correct: I need to do my homework before class.
mistakes.note: Academic tasks like homework or research use 'do' [17, 18].

mistakes.incorrect: The manager is making a lot of work today.
mistakes.correct: The manager is doing a lot of work today.
mistakes.note: 'Making work' implies creating unnecessary tasks for others [18].

mistakes.incorrect: We should make a research on solar energy.
mistakes.correct: We should do/conduct research on solar energy.
mistakes.note: Research is always 'done' or 'conducted' [17, 19].

mistakes.incorrect: The workers decided to make a strike.
mistakes.correct: The workers decided to go on strike.
mistakes.note: Industrial action is expressed as 'go on strike' [20].

mistakes.incorrect: Technology makes a significant role in life.
mistakes.correct: Technology plays a significant role in life.
mistakes.note: The standard collocation is 'play a role' [21].

mistakes.incorrect: Please make an appointment with the doctor.
mistakes.correct: Please book/make an appointment with the doctor.
mistakes.note: 'Make' is acceptable, but 'book' is common for professional services [22].

mistakes.incorrect: He made a mistake in the calculation.
mistakes.correct: He made a mistake in the calculation.
mistakes.note: Use 'make' for errors, but ensure the preposition is 'in' [23].

mistakes.incorrect: The air pollution are getting worse in cities.
mistakes.correct: The air pollution is getting worse in cities.
mistakes.note: Pollution is uncountable and takes a singular verb [24].

mistakes.incorrect: The information provided are very useful.
mistakes.correct: The information provided is very useful.
mistakes.note: Information is strictly uncountable [25].

mistakes.incorrect: Overpopulation lead to resource depletion.
mistakes.correct: Overpopulation leads to resource depletion.
mistakes.note: Uncountable nouns follow singular verb rules [26, 27].

mistakes.incorrect: Our research show a rise in temperatures.
mistakes.correct: Our research shows a rise in temperatures.
mistakes.note: Research is uncountable; avoid 'researches' [19].

mistakes.incorrect: The waste disposal are not efficient here.
mistakes.correct: The waste disposal is not efficient here.
mistakes.note: Disposal is a singular concept/uncountable [28, 29].

mistakes.incorrect: New software were installed on the laptop.
mistakes.correct: New software was installed on the laptop.
mistakes.note: Software is an uncountable noun [6, 30].

mistakes.incorrect: The homework given by teachers are hard.
mistakes.correct: The homework given by teachers is hard [31, 32].
mistakes.note: Homework is uncountable; do not use 'are' [31, 32].

mistakes.incorrect: You should lead a healthily lifestyle.
mistakes.correct: You should lead a healthy lifestyle.
mistakes.note: Use the adjective 'healthy' to modify the noun 'lifestyle' [33, 34].

mistakes.incorrect: A variety of vegetables is a nutritiously meal.
mistakes.correct: A variety of vegetables is a nutritious meal.
mistakes.note: Modify 'meal' with the adjective 'nutritious' [35, 36].

mistakes.incorrect: Environmental degrade is a serious challenge.
mistakes.correct: Environmental degradation is a serious challenge.
mistakes.note: Use the noun 'degradation' after the adjective 'environmental' [9].

mistakes.incorrect: This company sells high-quality produce.
mistakes.correct: This company sells high-quality products.
mistakes.note: 'Produce' (n) usually refers to agricultural goods; 'products' is for manufactured goods [6].

mistakes.incorrect: Academic achieve is important for students.
mistakes.correct: Academic achievement is important for students.
mistakes.note: Use the noun form 'achievement' for the subject [37, 38].

mistakes.incorrect: The city is develop quickly in terms of tech.
mistakes.correct: The city is developing quickly in terms of tech.
mistakes.note: Use the continuous form for ongoing processes [6, 39].

mistakes.incorrect: Digital device are very popular nowadays.
mistakes.correct: Digital devices are very popular nowadays.
mistakes.note: Nouns must agree with the plural verb 'are' [40].

mistakes.incorrect: We should avoid to use single-use plastics.
mistakes.correct: We should avoid using single-use plastics.
mistakes.note: The verb 'avoid' must be followed by V-ing [41, 42].

mistakes.incorrect: I plan enrolling in a business course.
mistakes.correct: I plan to enroll in a business course.
mistakes.note: 'Plan' is followed by a to-infinitive [7, 43].

mistakes.incorrect: He decided dropping out of college.
mistakes.correct: He decided to drop out of college.
mistakes.note: 'Decide' takes a to-infinitive [7, 44].

mistakes.incorrect: Teachers encourage students participating.
mistakes.correct: Teachers encourage students to participate.
mistakes.note: The pattern is 'encourage someone to do something' [7].

mistakes.incorrect: Can you imagine to live without internet?
mistakes.correct: Can you imagine living without internet?
mistakes.note: 'Imagine' is followed by a gerund [45].

mistakes.incorrect: He risks to lose his job after the scandal.
mistakes.correct: He risks losing his job after the scandal.
mistakes.note: 'Risk' requires a gerund (V-ing) [46].

mistakes.incorrect: I am looking forward to meet you.
mistakes.correct: I am looking forward to meeting you.
mistakes.note: The phrase 'look forward to' is followed by a gerund [40].

mistakes.incorrect: I have finished my assignment yesterday.
mistakes.correct: I finished my assignment yesterday.
mistakes.note: Use past simple for specific finished times [47, 48].

mistakes.incorrect: Human activity caused global warming so far.
mistakes.correct: Human activity has caused global warming so far.
mistakes.note: Use present perfect for actions continuing to the present [48, 49].

mistakes.incorrect: In 2020, the temperature has risen sharply.
mistakes.correct: In 2020, the temperature rose sharply.
mistakes.note: Specific years require the past simple tense [13].

mistakes.incorrect: He is a vegetarian since 1965.
mistakes.correct: He has been a vegetarian since 1965.
mistakes.note: Use present perfect with 'since' [50].

mistakes.incorrect: I saw an ad on TV at the moment.
mistakes.correct: I am seeing/watching an ad on TV at the moment.
mistakes.note: 'At the moment' requires a continuous tense [6].

mistakes.incorrect: By the time I arrived, he left already.
mistakes.correct: By the time I arrived, he had left already.
mistakes.note: Past perfect is used for the earlier of two past actions [51].

mistakes.incorrect: I graduated since three years.
mistakes.correct: I graduated three years ago.
mistakes.note: 'Ago' is used for finished time; 'since' is for a point in time [47].

mistakes.incorrect: I need to catch on with the class.
mistakes.correct: I need to catch up with the class.
mistakes.note: The correct particle is 'up' for reaching a standard [52, 53].

mistakes.incorrect: Students must hand out their homework today.
mistakes.correct: Students must hand in their homework today.
mistakes.note: 'Hand in' means submit; 'hand out' means distribute [53, 54].

mistakes.incorrect: I read into the case before the seminar.
mistakes.correct: I read up on the case before the seminar.
mistakes.note: Use 'read up on' to find information about a subject [53, 55].

mistakes.incorrect: She dropped from school after one year.
mistakes.correct: She dropped out of school after one year.
mistakes.note: The full phrase is 'drop out of' [44, 53].

mistakes.incorrect: Don't forget to sign for the yoga class.
mistakes.correct: Don't forget to sign up for the yoga class.
mistakes.note: Use 'sign up for' to join an activity [56].

mistakes.incorrect: I always go across my notes before tests.
mistakes.correct: I always go over my notes before tests.
mistakes.note: 'Go over' means to check or review [53].

mistakes.incorrect: I need to study on the new materials.
mistakes.correct: I need to study up on the new materials.
mistakes.note: Like 'read up on', use 'study up on' for research [55].

mistakes.incorrect: I saw the news on internet.
mistakes.correct: I saw the news on the internet.
mistakes.note: Global entities like 'internet' always require 'the' [30, 57, 58].

mistakes.incorrect: Environment is being destroyed by humans.
mistakes.correct: The environment is being destroyed by humans.
mistakes.note: Use 'the environment' in a general academic sense [11].

mistakes.incorrect: Sun heats up the Earth's surface.
mistakes.correct: The Sun heats up the Earth's surface.
mistakes.note: Unique celestial bodies require 'the' [59].

mistakes.incorrect: He earned degree in economics.
mistakes.correct: He earned a degree in economics.
mistakes.note: Singular countable nouns like 'degree' need an article [60].

mistakes.incorrect: Air pollution is a major health concern.
mistakes.correct: Air pollution is a major health concern.
mistakes.note: Abstract concepts generally don't use 'the' [61].

mistakes.incorrect: I have an interest on music.
mistakes.correct: I have an interest in music.
mistakes.note: The idiom is 'have an interest in' [62].

mistakes.incorrect: She is the teacher's pet.
mistakes.correct: She is the teacher's pet.
mistakes.note: Ensure the possessive apostrophe is used correctly [63].

mistakes.incorrect: The computer is currently not doing.
mistakes.correct: The computer is currently not working/running.
mistakes.note: Use 'working' for machines/software [64, 65].

mistakes.incorrect: I need to change my mobile phone electrification.
mistakes.correct: I need to use my mobile phone charger.
mistakes.note: The device for charging is a 'charger' [66].

mistakes.incorrect: My phone is out of battery power.
mistakes.correct: My phone battery is flat/low.
mistakes.note: 'Flat' is a common adjective for dead batteries [67].

mistakes.incorrect: This app has a user-easy interface.
mistakes.correct: This app has a user-friendly interface.
mistakes.note: The academic term is 'user-friendly' [30, 68].

mistakes.incorrect: I forgot to back the files up.
mistakes.correct: I forgot to back up the files.
mistakes.note: While separable, 'back up' is the standard verb for copying data [30, 69].

mistakes.incorrect: The system crashed because of a bug inside.
mistakes.correct: The system crashed because of a bug in the software.
mistakes.note: Use the preposition 'in' for bugs in systems [70].

mistakes.incorrect: I need to find a new software sub-part.
mistakes.correct: I need to find a new software component.
mistakes.note: The technical term is 'component' [71].

mistakes.incorrect: There are too much people in the city.
mistakes.correct: There are too many people in the city.
mistakes.note: People is a plural countable noun [72].

mistakes.incorrect: He doesn't have many knowledge about IT.
mistakes.correct: He doesn't have much knowledge about IT.
mistakes.note: Knowledge is uncountable [25].

mistakes.incorrect: How many money do you earn a month?
mistakes.correct: How much money do you earn a month?
mistakes.note: Money is uncountable [25].

mistakes.incorrect: We should eat less salt and more many fruit.
mistakes.correct: We should eat less salt and more fruit.
mistakes.note: Use 'more' directly with uncountable/countable nouns [73].

mistakes.incorrect: There is many air pollution in Hanoi.
mistakes.correct: There is a lot of/much air pollution in Hanoi.
mistakes.note: Pollution is uncountable [24].

mistakes.incorrect: Too many fast food is bad for health.
mistakes.correct: Too much fast food is bad for health.
mistakes.note: Food is typically treated as uncountable here [42].

mistakes.incorrect: I saw many informative advertisements.
mistakes.correct: I saw many informative advertisements.
mistakes.note: 'Advertisement' is countable, but 'advertising' is not [74].

mistakes.incorrect: I feel very weak after the accident.
mistakes.correct: I am still weak after the accident.
mistakes.note: Use 'still' to show a continued state of being [50].

mistakes.incorrect: The doctor is talking Tom's temperature.
mistakes.correct: The doctor is taking Tom's temperature.
mistakes.note: The correct verb is 'take a temperature' [75].

mistakes.incorrect: I got sunburnam on the beach.
mistakes.correct: I got sunburn on the beach.
mistakes.note: Avoid spelling errors for 'sunburn' [76].

mistakes.incorrect: Reading in soft light can hurt eyes.
mistakes.correct: Reading in dim light can hurt your eyes.
mistakes.note: 'Dim light' is the correct term for low visibility [35, 77].

mistakes.incorrect: He is in the pink health.
mistakes.correct: He is in the pink of health.
mistakes.note: The full idiom is 'in the pink of health' [78].

mistakes.incorrect: You should use eye drops for dry eyes.
mistakes.correct: You should use eye drops for dry eyes.
mistakes.note: Ensure 'eye drops' is two words/compound [77].

mistakes.incorrect: Eating sugar can causes acne.
mistakes.correct: Eating sugar can cause acne.
mistakes.note: Modal verbs like 'can' are followed by the bare infinitive [77].

mistakes.incorrect: Kids are dropping out of school.
mistakes.correct: Children/Students are dropping out of school.
mistakes.note: In IELTS Writing, avoid 'kids' [79].

mistakes.incorrect: I want to get some Z's now.
mistakes.correct: I need to get some sleep.
mistakes.note: 'Catch some Z's' is an idiom for Speaking only [78].

mistakes.incorrect: The job is a piece of cake.
mistakes.correct: The job is very easy/manageable.
mistakes.note: Avoid informal idioms in academic tasks [36, 43].

mistakes.incorrect: The boss was happy with the report.
mistakes.correct: The manager/director was satisfied with the report.
mistakes.note: Use 'manager' or 'director' for a more formal tone [2].

mistakes.incorrect: I'm under a lot of pressure at my new job.
mistakes.correct: I am experiencing considerable pressure at my workplace.
mistakes.note: Use 'considerable' instead of 'a lot of' in formal writing [80].

mistakes.incorrect: I'll resend the artwork ASAP.
mistakes.correct: I will resend the artwork as soon as possible.
mistakes.note: Avoid acronyms like ASAP in formal business emails [81].

mistakes.incorrect: Can you give me some tips?
mistakes.correct: Could you provide some advice/recommendations?
mistakes.note: Use 'advice' or 'recommendations' in formal contexts [24, 25].

mistakes.incorrect: My sister is a nurse but she works in a clinic.
mistakes.correct: My sister is a nurse, but she works in a clinic.
mistakes.note: Use a comma before coordinating conjunctions like 'but' [82].

mistakes.incorrect: My teeth hurt so I make an appointment.
mistakes.correct: My teeth hurt, so I make an appointment.
mistakes.note: A comma is mandatory before 'so' in a compound sentence [82, 83].

mistakes.incorrect: He is handsome but he lacks personality.
mistakes.correct: He is handsome, but he seems to lack personality.
mistakes.note: Always separate independent clauses with a comma + conjunction [84].

mistakes.incorrect: You should wear a hat or you get sunburn.
mistakes.correct: You should wear a hat, or you might get sunburn.
mistakes.note: Use a comma before 'or' [82].

mistakes.incorrect: I am thirsty but I don't have money.
mistakes.correct: I am thirsty, but I don't have any money.
mistakes.note: Punctuation check for 'but' [84].

mistakes.incorrect: The singer is ill so he won't perform.
mistakes.correct: The singer is ill, so he won't perform.
mistakes.note: Result clauses with 'so' need a comma [84].

mistakes.incorrect: Tomorrow is a holiday and I will sleep more.
mistakes.correct: Tomorrow is a holiday, and I will sleep more.
mistakes.note: Adding information with 'and' needs a comma [84].

mistakes.incorrect: He is a famous science.
mistakes.correct: He is a famous scientist.
mistakes.note: Use '-ist' suffix for the person specializing in science [85, 86].

mistakes.incorrect: The biology studied the plants.
mistakes.correct: The biologist studied the plants.
mistakes.note: A 'biologist' is the researcher [86, 87].

mistakes.incorrect: She wants to be a physics.
mistakes.correct: She wants to be a physicist.
mistakes.note: A 'physicist' is a person, 'physics' is the subject [86, 87].

mistakes.incorrect: The architecture designed the bridge.
mistakes.correct: The architect designed the bridge.
mistakes.note: An 'architect' is the person [86, 88].

mistakes.incorrect: He works as a chemistry in the lab.
mistakes.correct: He works as a chemist in the lab.
mistakes.note: A 'chemist' is the professional [87, 89].

mistakes.incorrect: The history wrote a book about Rome.
mistakes.correct: The historian wrote a book about Rome.
mistakes.note: An 'historian' writes about history [86].

mistakes.incorrect: She is a talented piano.
mistakes.correct: She is a talented pianist.
mistakes.note: Add '-ist' to the instrument to name the player [90, 91].

mistakes.incorrect: If you will heat ice, it melts.
mistakes.correct: If you heat ice, it melts.
mistakes.note: Zero conditional uses present simple in both clauses [83].

mistakes.incorrect: If you don't wear a hat, you get sunburn.
mistakes.correct: If you don't wear a hat, you will get sunburn.
mistakes.note: First conditional uses 'will' in the result clause [75].

mistakes.incorrect: If I am the president, I would prioritize nature.
mistakes.correct: If I were the president, I would prioritize nature.
mistakes.note: Second conditional (unreal) uses 'were' [92].

mistakes.incorrect: If I would have time, I'd go out.
mistakes.correct: If I had time, I would go out.
mistakes.note: Never use 'would' in the 'if' clause [83].

mistakes.incorrect: If she studied hard, she aced the test.
mistakes.correct: If she had studied hard, she would have aced the test.
mistakes.note: Third conditional is for unreal past events [83].

mistakes.incorrect: If we protect biodiversity, life stays stable.
mistakes.correct: If we protect biodiversity, life stays stable.
mistakes.note: General truths take the zero conditional [11].

mistakes.incorrect: If you take vitamins, you stay healthy.
mistakes.correct: If you take vitamins, you will stay healthy.
mistakes.note: Specific future outcome requires 'will' [93].

mistakes.incorrect: I want to purchase a return ticket.
mistakes.correct: I want to buy a return ticket.
mistakes.note: In spoken English, 'buy' is more natural than 'purchase' [94].

mistakes.incorrect: The city is very famous for its tech.
mistakes.correct: The city is renowned for its tech.
mistakes.note: 'Renowned' is a more positive, academic synonym for 'famous' [94, 95].

mistakes.incorrect: We need to delay the meeting.
mistakes.correct: We need to put off the meeting.
mistakes.note: 'Put off' is a more natural phrasal verb synonym [94].

mistakes.incorrect: Hazardous waste is very dangerous.
mistakes.correct: Hazardous waste is dangerous.
mistakes.note: Avoid using both synonyms in one phrase; 'hazardous' is more academic [96, 97].

mistakes.incorrect: The price of the phone is substantial.
mistakes.correct: The price of the phone is high/significant.
mistakes.note: 'Substantial' usually refers to amounts/changes, not simple price tags [98, 99].

mistakes.incorrect: I don't mind what you do.
mistakes.correct: I agree with what you do.
mistakes.note: Use 'I agree' instead of 'I don't mind' to express clear support [100].

mistakes.incorrect: He is a copy cat who steals ideas.
mistakes.correct: He is an unoriginal person who steals ideas.
mistakes.note: 'Copy cat' is informal; avoid it in academic writing [101].

mistakes.incorrect: A plastic small green bottle.
mistakes.correct: A small green plastic bottle.
mistakes.note: Order: Size (small) > Color (green) > Material (plastic) [102].

mistakes.incorrect: A wooden old beautiful house.
mistakes.correct: A beautiful old wooden house.
mistakes.note: Order: Opinion (beautiful) > Age (old) > Material (wooden) [102].

mistakes.incorrect: A digital new fast device.
mistakes.correct: A fast new digital device.
mistakes.note: Order: Opinion/Quality (fast) > Age (new) > Type (digital) [102].

mistakes.incorrect: Blue large round eyes.
mistakes.correct: Large round blue eyes.
mistakes.note: Order: Size (large) > Shape (round) > Color (blue) [102].

mistakes.incorrect: A leather black expensive jacket.
mistakes.correct: An expensive black leather jacket.
mistakes.note: Order: Opinion (expensive) > Color (black) > Material (leather) [102].

mistakes.incorrect: An innovative useful software app.
mistakes.correct: A useful innovative software app.
mistakes.note: Order: Quality (useful) > Age/Innovation (innovative) [68, 102].

mistakes.incorrect: Mountain clean fresh air.
mistakes.correct: Clean fresh mountain air.
mistakes.note: Opinion/Quality comes before location/origin [102].

mistakes.incorrect: Lack of sleep effects your health.
mistakes.correct: Lack of sleep affects your health.
mistakes.note: 'Affect' is the verb; 'effect' is the noun [42, 47].

mistakes.incorrect: Technology has a positive affect.
mistakes.correct: Technology has a positive effect.
mistakes.note: 'Effect' is the noun form [21, 103].

mistakes.incorrect: I have near finished my research.
mistakes.correct: I have nearly finished my research.
mistakes.note: 'Nearly' means almost; 'near' refers to distance [25].

mistakes.incorrect: I need to check my mailbox frequently.
mistakes.correct: I need to check my mailbox frequently.
mistakes.note: 'Mailbox' is usually one word [104].

mistakes.incorrect: The company has achieved targets.
mistakes.correct: The company has achieved its targets.
mistakes.note: Ensure spelling of 'achieved' is correct (i before e) [37, 105].

mistakes.incorrect: The forest was contaminated with toxin.
mistakes.correct: The forest was contaminated with toxic chemicals.
mistakes.note: 'Toxic' is the adjective required here [9].

mistakes.incorrect: I saw an informative advertisment.
mistakes.correct: I saw an informative advertisement.
mistakes.note: Note the correct spelling with 'e' before 'ment' [2, 6].

mistakes.incorrect: Many trees have been cut up recently.
mistakes.correct: Many trees have been cut down recently.
mistakes.note: Use 'cut down' for felling trees [13, 59].

mistakes.incorrect: The sun is heating the Earth out.
mistakes.correct: The sun is heating up the Earth's surface.
mistakes.note: The correct particle is 'up' [59].

mistakes.incorrect: The oil had all been used off.
mistakes.correct: The oil had all been used up.
mistakes.note: 'Use up' means to exhaust a supply [59].

mistakes.incorrect: We should throw rubbish off carefully.
mistakes.correct: We should not throw rubbish away carelessly.
mistakes.note: The phrasal verb is 'throw away' [59, 106].

mistakes.incorrect: They helped clean the beaches out.
mistakes.correct: They helped clean up the beaches.
mistakes.note: Use 'clean up' for removing pollution/dirt [13, 59].

mistakes.incorrect: Rivers have dried off due to drought.
mistakes.correct: Rivers have dried up due to drought.
mistakes.note: The correct phrasal verb is 'dry up' [13, 59].

mistakes.incorrect: Disasters wiped the properties off.
mistakes.correct: Disasters wiped out many properties.
mistakes.note: 'Wipe out' means to destroy completely [59].
